---
title: "Stability and comparability of meritocratic beliefs in school-age students: A measurement invariance approach across time and cohorts"
#subtitle: "{{< words-sum body-note >}} words"
format:
  html: 
    code-fold: true
    html-math-method: katex
    number-sections: true
    code-link: true
    title-block-banner: true
    theme:
      - pretty.scss
  pdf: 
    geometry: margin=2cm
    template-partials: 
      - title.tex
    keep-tex: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage[noblocks]{authblk}
        \renewcommand*{\Authsep}{, }
        \renewcommand*{\Authand}{, }
        \renewcommand*{\Authands}{, }
        \renewcommand\Affilfont{\small}
    number-sections: true
  wordcount-pdf: 
    wordcount-banner: true
#params:
#  wordcount: |
#    <strong>{{< words-sum body-note-ref >}} total #words</strong>: {{< words-body >}} in the body • {{< #words-ref #>}} in the references • {{< words-note >}} #in the notes
editor: source
bibliography: "input/bib/merit-factorial.bib"
csl: "input/bib/sage-harvard.csl"
author:
  - name: Andreas Laffert
    corresponding: true
    orcid: 0000-0002-9008-2454
    email: alaffertt@estudiante.uc.cl 
    affiliations:
      - ref: 1
  - name: Juan Carlos Castillo
    orcid: 0000-0003-1265-7854
    email: juancastillov@uchile.cl
    affiliations:
      - ref: 2
  - name: René Canales
    orcid: 0009-0002-4299-2725
    email: renecanales@uc.cl
    affiliations:
      - ref: 1    
  - name: Tomás Urzúa
    orcid: 0009-0008-3167-6819
    email: tomas.urzua@ug.chile.cl
    affiliations:
      - ref: 2    
  - name: Kevin Carrasco
    orcid: 0000-0002-9348-8361
    email: kevin.carrasco@uchile.cl
    affiliations:
      - ref: 2 
affiliations: 
  - id: 1
    name: Institute of Sociology, Pontifical Catholic University of Chile
  - id: 2
    name: Department of Sociology, University of Chile
citeproc: true
abstract: | 
 Meritocratic beliefs shape how adolescents understand inequality and the legitimacy of social arrangements. Schools are key sites of meritocratic socialization, making adolescence a crucial period for studying how these beliefs are formed. However, research on adolescents’ meritocratic beliefs has often relied on unidimensional measures that conflate perceptions of how allocation works with preferences about how it should work and treat meritocratic and non-meritocratic principles as opposites. This study tests a multidimensional model that distinguishes between perceived and preferred meritocracy and non-meritocracy. Using two-wave panel survey data from Chilean students in 8th and 10th grade (Wave 1: N = 846; Wave 2: N = 662), we assess whether these dimensions can be distinguished, compared across school stages, and measured equivalently over time. Results support a four-factor structure and show that endorsement of merit can coexist with recognition of non-meritocratic advantage. The scale achieves strict longitudinal invariance within students but not across school levels.
  \newline
 **Keywords**: Meritocracy · Schools · Measurement invariance · Socialization · Chile
link-citations: true
linestretch: 1.5       
mainfont: Times New Roman
fontsize: 13pt          
colorlinks: true
fig-height: 4
fig-width: 7.5
---

:::: {.justify}

{{< include 01-introduction.qmd >}}

{{< include 02-antecedentes.qmd >}}

{{< include 03-methods.qmd >}}

{{< include 04-measurement-model.qmd >}}

{{< include 04-invariance.qmd >}}

{{< include 05-discussion.qmd >}}

{{< include 06-conclusion.qmd >}}

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
::::
